title: practical english usage - Part 300
content:
I haven't been to the theatre for ages.

Granny has been to see us twice since Christmas.

Been is often used as a past participle of come and go.

395 been      meaning       'come'    or  'gone' For other uses of the words discussed in this section, see a good dictionary.

Keep smiling whatever happens.

Keep can be used before -ing forms.

Promise me you will always stay/remain my little boy.

Stay and remain are also sometimes used before noun phrases.

I hope you will always remain so charming.

How do you manage to stay young and fit?  Keep calm.

adjectives. Remain is more formal.

To  talk about things not changing, we  can use stay, keep or remain before 10 no   change:     stay,     keep,     remain Could you shorten the sleeves on this jacket?

The weather's beginning to brighten up. His eyes narrowed.

The fog thickened.             They're widening the road here.

‘ get more ...’  or ‘ make more .. .'. Many of them end in -en. Examples: A number of verbs which  are related to adjectives have meanings like 9 verbs     related      to  adjectives:       thicken,        brighten,         etc Fall is used to mean ‘ become' in fall ill, fall asleep and fall in love.

8  fall    ill,  etc Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  397   before (preposition) and in front of Damn! It's starting to rain.

We will begin the meeting with a message from the President.

We   generally prefer begin when we  are using a more formal style. Compare: If Hannah doesn't come soon, let's begin/start without her.

I began/started teaching when I was 24.

Begin and start can both be used with the same meaning.

1  meaning;      formality 398 begin      and   start     ______________                  _______ For before as an adverb, ►    396.   For by meaning 'at/on or before', >414.

For the difference between in front of and facing/opposite, ►    549.      For before as a conjunction, ►     250.

c  in the expressions right before one's eyes, before one's very eyes.

I came up before the magistrates for dangerous driving last week.

to mean 'in the presence of (somebody important)’ We use 'a' before a consonant and 'an' before a vowel.

Her name comes before mine in the alphabet.

Do you mind? I was before / in front of you!

to talk about order in queues, lists, documents, etc Before is normally used to refer to time. However, it can refer to place: not It's parked in front of the station, (     .. . before the station.) I must move my car before nine o'clock.

397 before       (preposition)        and   in   front      of For the difference between before and ever, ►    454.1.   For before as a conjunction, ►    250.

(     .. .-JhH4^eforer4-waHtr-to4mveL) I want to get married one day. But before that / first, I want to travel.

Instead we  use first or before that.

Before is not generally used alone to mean 'first’  or 'before that happens’ .

3 before,       before       that     and   first not I left school four years ago. (     .. .-four-yea rs-before / before four years) To  count back from the present, we  use ago, not before (►   362).

was different. (    .. . thaI-4 had4ffi4Kfor^t4ght years ...) When I went back to the town that I had left eightyears before, everything normally used.

to say how  much  earlier something else had happened. A  past perfect tense is We   also use before after a time expression to 'count back’  from a past moment - 2 counting       back    from    a  past    time:   eight      years      before vocabulary  •  399   below, under, underneath and beneath not He's still under 18. (     .. .-underneath-18.) There's a mouse under(neath) the piano.

physical position. Compare: Underneath is sometimes used as a preposition instead of under, but only for 6 underneath You can't see this film if you're under 18.

There were under twenty people at the lecture.

We   usually use under, not below, to mean ‘ less/fewer than' or ‘ younger than'.

5 'less   than':   under She's well below average in intelligence.

The plane came down below the clouds.

Parts of Holland are below sea level.
